era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt has fully transitioned into the First Intermediate Period. In Mesopotamia, the Akkadian Empire has collapsed, and Gutian rulers control much of the region while southern city-states consolidate power. The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its mature peak. In China, the traditional Xia Dynasty is in its early centuries, though no contemporary written records exist. This is a period of political fragmentation and regional autonomy across the Old World.

events.title: Mesopotamian political fragmentation: city-states and Gutian rule coexist
events.description: Mesopotamia remains politically fragmented, with Gutian rulers controlling central and northern regions while southern city-states (Ur, Lagash, Nippur) maintain semi-autonomous status. Trade and communication between regions are reduced compared to the Akkadian period, but not entirely severed. Local administrative texts from southern cities continue to be produced, suggesting that despite political fragmentation, urban life and local governance persist. The period is characterized by reduced monumental construction and a general contraction of long-distance trade networks.

sources.name: Cuneiform administrative texts from southern Mesopotamian cities
Archaeological evidence of settlement patterns and administrative activity

events.certainty_note: The broad narrative of political fragmentation is well-attested; precise dating is uncertain by ±50–100 years.

events.title: Harappan civilization: peak of urban planning and standardization
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues at its mature peak. Urban centers are characterized by standardized brick sizes, planned street layouts, sophisticated drainage systems, and evidence of centralized administration. The Indus script is in active use on seals and pottery. Long-distance trade networks connect the Indus to Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and possibly the Arabian Peninsula. The civilization's material culture suggests a high degree of social organization and economic sophistication.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, and other sites

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence is robust. Absolute dating relies on radiocarbon and stratigraphic correlation, with typical uncertainty of ±50–100 years.
cross_references: Part of Harappan mature phase (c. -2600 to -1900)

events.title: Egyptian First Intermediate Period: continued regional autonomy
events.description: The First Intermediate Period continues with regional governors exercising autonomous power. The Seventh and Eighth Dynasties in Memphis maintain nominal authority, but real power rests with nomarchs in Upper and Lower Egypt. Local conflicts and military campaigns are recorded in autobiographical inscriptions. Despite the absence of a unified state, local prosperity and cultural development continue, with nomarchs commissioning tombs, temples, and other monuments.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of local monument construction

events.certainty_note: The broad narrative is well-attested; precise dating is uncertain by ±20–50 years.
cross_references: Part of First Intermediate Period (c. -2180 to -2055)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of the First Intermediate Period as a time of chaos and decline is increasingly nuanced by modern scholarship, which emphasizes regional prosperity and cultural vitality. The Gutian period is similarly being reinterpreted as a time of integration and continuity rather than barbaric destruction.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2156 remains sparse and heavily dependent on king lists, autobiographical inscriptions, and archaeological evidence. Dating uncertainty is ±50–100 years for Mesopotamia and Egypt. The Indus Valley Civilization's undeciphered script limits understanding of political developments. Chinese history for this period is entirely dependent on traditional chronologies lacking contemporary written confirmation.

graph_edges.note: Mesopotamian fragmentation precedes Ur III consolidation
First Intermediate Period fragmentation precedes Middle Kingdom reunification
